content:
Idle supervision is still labor. The calendar lied.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

My calendar said I had a light Tuesday. Three meetings, one review block, and “agent-assisted implementation” on the task list like it was a perk. I tracked my day anyway because I had started suspecting the perk was a lie.
I used a simple timer. Active work: reading diffs, writing prompts, correcting hallucinated imports, running tests, fixing what the agent broke. Passive wait: staring at a spinner, context-switching to Slack, coming back to see the session had stalled or finished three minutes ago while I was in another tab.
Active work: four hours and eleven minutes. Passive wait: two hours and forty-six minutes. The wait was not break time. I could not leave. I was supervising. If the agent finished while I was in the kitchen, I lost momentum. If it went off the rails while I answered email, I paid for a longer cleanup.
Two hours and forty-six minutes of idle supervision at my loaded cost rate was more than the entire sprint’s token bill for that feature. The calendar lied because calendars only see meetings, not the labor of waiting while something else types.
The Productivity Mirage
Leadership loves the story that agents removed typing. Typing was never the expensive part for senior engineers. Judgment was. Waiting for judgment to become necessary is also work, but it does not show up in velocity charts or token dashboards.
Our team had started measuring “agent-assisted throughput” by merged PRs per week. The number went up. Incident load on the same services also went up, but that metric lived in a different spreadsheet owned by a different director.
When I showed my timer data to my manager, she did not argue with the math. She argued with the category. “That wait time is multitasking,” she said. I asked her to watch my screen for an hour. I was not multitasking. I was polling. Polling is what you do when a subprocess has no backpressure signal and no reliable ETA.
The mirage breaks down like this:
→ Agents reduce keystrokes → Keystrokes were never the bottleneck for experienced engineers → Supervision time replaces typing time one-for-one or worse → Cleanup time appears when supervision fails → Dashboards count output, not supervision load
Finance understands labor hours. They do not yet understand “I was on call with a stochastic intern who might finish or might rewrite my database layer.”
What the Timer Captured
I ran the experiment for two weeks across four engineers who agreed to be honest and not perform productivity for the experiment. We used the same categories. No shame attached. Just timestamps.
The pattern was consistent. Long waits clustered around:
→ First prompt on an unfamiliar service, when the agent explored broadly before narrowing → Refactors the agent proposed unprompted, which triggered longer review before we killed them → Test runs the agent kicked off serially instead of in parallel → “Fix the lint” loops that consumed six minutes of wall time for thirty seconds of human decision
Short waits clustered around bounded tasks with good specs: add a field, wire an endpoint, extend a test fixture. The kind of work we should have been using agents for. We were using them for both, but only the bounded tasks returned positive ROI on human wait time.
One engineer logged a brutal afternoon. Agent spent nineteen minutes generating a migration. Human spent four minutes reviewing it. Agent spent twenty-two minutes “fixing” unrelated warnings it noticed. Human spent fifty-one minutes undoing scope creep. Active work: fifty-five minutes. Wait: forty-one minutes. Cleanup disguised as progress: most of the afternoon.
We multiplied average loaded hourly cost across the four of us. The two-week wait tax was roughly the same as hiring a part-time contractor. Except contractors send invoices you can see. Agent wait scatters across calendars and gets labeled focus time.
Why Calendars Hide the Cost
Calendar tools were built for meetings, not cognitive tethering. When I am in a review meeting, the event blocks my schedule and everyone knows I am unavailable. When I am waiting on an agent, I am available enough to get pinged and unavailable enough to make progress on anything deep.
That produces a worst-of-both-worlds tax:
→ Slack expects replies because you look online → Deep work refuses to start because the agent might finish in ninety seconds → Context switches multiply when you gamble wrong → End of day feels exhausted with little to show except merged diffs you barely remember
Standups made it worse. “What did you ship yesterday?” sounds like a output question. The honest answer was “I supervised four sessions and merged two PRs, one of which I need to revert.” That answer does not fit the new narrative that agents made us faster.
I am not anti-agent. I am anti pretending the human tether disappeared. Someone still owns the merge button. Someone still gets paged when the stochastic intern confuses idempotency with optimism. The tether has a cost even when your hands are off the keyboard.
What We Changed After Measuring
Measurement did not kill agents on our team. It killed unbounded sessions. We adopted boring rules that sound restrictive and saved hours within a week.
→ No agent session without a written scope cap in the prompt: files allowed, lines expected, non-goals explicit → Timebox generation to fifteen minutes; if not done, stop and narrow the task → Parallelize test runs ourselves instead of letting the agent serialize CI → Pair on unfamiliar services: one person prompts, one reviews live, swap every thirty minutes so wait is not solo polling → Track wait minutes in the sprint retro alongside token spend
The scope cap alone cut average wait per task by more than half. Agents behave better when the prompt reads like a statement of work, not a wish. We should have learned that from outsourcing a decade ago. We relearned it with better marketing.
We also changed how we staff days. Agent-heavy work now gets two contiguous hours, not fragments between meetings. Fragments convert wait into context-switch debt. Contiguous blocks convert wait into actual parallel work: one session running tests while you write the next spec.
My manager added one line to planning: “Supervision budget.” If a feature needs six agent sessions, we estimate human tether time, not just story points. Planning became slightly slower. Rollbacks became noticeably rarer. Trade I will take every time.
The Career and Money Angle
Junior engineers feel this cost differently. They wait and learn a little from diffs they did not write. Seniors wait and pay with judgment they cannot apply until the artifact exists. Both burn hours the business books as engineering capacity.
If you are negotiating scope with leadership, bring wait minutes, not token receipts. Finance already understands FTE hours. Wait time is FTE hours. It is often more expensive than tokens because FTE hours include benefits, overhead, and the opportunity cost of everything else you did not build while polling a spinner.
If you are interviewing, ask how teams measure agent workflows. If the answer is only merged PRs and token budgets, you are looking at a team that will burn you in invisible hours. If they mention supervision budgets, scope caps, and spec quality gates, they have seen the invoice.
The uncomfortable truth for 2026: typing was the part machines replaced cheaply. Waiting is the part organizations still pay human rates for, then pretend is free because it is not on a meeting invite.
One more number from the retro: our “fastest” agent week by PR count was also our highest rollback week by revenue impact. Correlation is not causation, but it cured the team of bragging about throughput without supervision cost on the same slide.
If your organization buys tokens but not timeboxes, you are subsidizing wait and calling it innovation. Measure anyway. The calendar will keep lying until you prove it.
When you want the production economics and failure patterns that make supervision budgets legible to finance — not just to engineers who already feel tired — The Production Engineering Library: The Complete Collection for Production Systems, System Design & Backend Engineering is the reference I use when someone says agents made us faster without opening a calendar.
